content:
# Luke Chapter 2 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), Folios 43v-44v (PDF pages 85-87) | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_002_page_001.jpg, LUK_002_page_002.jpg, LUK_002_page_003.jpg

**1** Around that time, Caesar Augustus issued an official decree that a census should be taken of the entire Roman world.

**2** This was the first registration of its kind, and it took place while Quirinius was governing Syria.

**3** So everyone had to travel to their own ancestral town to be registered.

**4** Joseph also went up from the town of Nazareth in Galilee to Judea, to Bethlehem — the city of David — because he was a descendant of David's royal line.

**5** He went to register along with Mary, who was pledged to him in marriage and was expecting a child.

**6** While they were there, the time came for her baby to be born.

**7** She gave birth to her firstborn son, wrapped him snugly in strips of cloth, and laid him in a feeding trough — because there was no room for them in the guest quarters.

**8** In the fields nearby, shepherds were staying out at night, keeping watch over their flocks.

**9** Suddenly an angel of the Lord stood before them, and the radiance of the Lord blazed all around them. They were absolutely terrified.

**10** But the angel said to them, "Don't be afraid! Listen — I am bringing you wonderful news that will be an overwhelming joy for all people:

**11** Today, in David's city, a Savior has been born for you — he is the Messiah, the Lord!

**12** And here is how you will recognize him: you will find a baby wrapped in cloth, lying in a feeding trough."

**13** All at once, a vast company of heaven's armies appeared with the angel, praising God and saying,

**14** "Glory to God in the highest heavens, and on earth peace among those on whom his favor rests!"

**15** When the angels had returned to heaven, the shepherds said to one another, "Let's go straight to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened — what the Lord has told us about!"

**16** So they hurried off and found Mary and Joseph, and the baby lying in the feeding trough.

**17** After they had seen him, they spread the word about everything the angel had told them about this child.

**18** Everyone who heard it was astonished at what the shepherds said.

**19** But Mary treasured all these things, turning them over carefully in her heart.

**20** The shepherds went back, glorifying and praising God for everything they had heard and seen — it was exactly as they had been told.

**21** When eight days had passed and it was time for the child's circumcision, he was given the name Jesus — the name the angel had spoken before he was even conceived.

**22** And when the time came for their purification according to the Law of Moses, they brought him up to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord —

**23** as it is written in the Lord's Law: "Every firstborn male shall be set apart as holy to the Lord" —

**24** and to offer the sacrifice required by the Lord's Law: "a pair of turtledoves or two young pigeons."

**25** Now there was a man in Jerusalem named Simeon. He was an upright and deeply devout man who was waiting expectantly for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit rested on him.

**26** The Holy Spirit had revealed to him that he would not die before he had seen the Lord's Messiah with his own eyes.

**27** Guided by the Spirit, he came into the temple courts. And when the parents brought in the child Jesus to carry out what the Law required,

**28** Simeon took the baby in his arms and praised God, saying,

**29** "Now, Sovereign Lord, you are releasing your servant in peace, just as you promised,

**30** because my own eyes have seen your salvation —

**31** which you have prepared openly before all peoples —

**32** a light that will bring revelation to the nations and will be the crowning glory of your people Israel."

**33** The child's father and mother were amazed at the things being said about him.

**34** Then Simeon blessed them and said to Mary, his mother, "Pay attention: this child is destined to cause the falling and rising of many in Israel, and to become a sign that will be fiercely opposed —

**35** yes, and a sword will pierce your own soul too — so that the hidden thoughts of many hearts will be laid bare."

**36** There was also a prophet named Anna, the daughter of Phanuel, from the tribe of Asher. She was very old, having lived with her husband for seven years after her marriage,

**37** and then as a widow until she was eighty-four. She never left the temple, serving God night and day with fasting and prayer.

**38** Coming up at that very moment, she gave thanks to God and began speaking about the child to everyone who was waiting for the redemption of Jerusalem.

**39** When they had completed everything required by the Lord's Law, they returned to Galilee, to their own town of Nazareth.

**40** And the child grew and became strong, filled with wisdom, and God's grace was upon him.

**41** Every year his parents traveled to Jerusalem for the Passover festival.

**42** When he was twelve years old, they went up as usual for the festival.

**43** After the celebration ended and they started home, the boy Jesus stayed behind in Jerusalem — but his parents did not realize it.

**44** Assuming he was somewhere in the traveling group, they went a full day's journey before they began searching for him among their relatives and friends.

**45** When they could not find him, they turned back to Jerusalem to look for him.

**46** After three days, they finally found him in the temple, sitting among the teachers, listening to them and asking them questions.

**47** Everyone who heard him was stunned by his understanding and his answers.

**48** When his parents saw him, they were shocked. His mother said to him, "Son, why have you done this to us? Your father and I have been worried sick, searching everywhere for you!"

**49** He said to them, "Why were you searching for me? Didn't you know that I had to be in my Father's house?"

**50** But they did not grasp what he meant by this.

**51** Then he went back down with them to Nazareth, and he was obedient to them. And his mother kept all these things stored in her heart.

**52** And Jesus kept growing — in wisdom, in stature, and in favor with God and with people.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **Verse 1 — "entire Roman world" for oikoumene:** The Greek oikoumene ("inhabited earth") referred to the Roman Empire's sphere. "Entire Roman world" captures both the political scope and the felt reality for Luke's audience — this was the known world under one power.

2. **Verse 7 — "feeding trough" for phatne and "guest quarters" for katalyma:** The Greek phatne means a manger or feeding trough for animals; I retain "feeding trough" to make the visceral contrast vivid. Katalyma is not a commercial inn (pandocheion, which Luke uses in 10:34) but a guest room in a home. "Guest quarters" communicates this more accurately than the traditional "inn."

3. **Verse 9 — "the radiance of the Lord blazed" for doxa kyriou perielamsen:** The doxa is not merely abstract glory but a visible, overwhelming brightness. "Blazed all around them" captures the sudden, engulfing nature of the divine light.

4. **Verse 14 — "among those on whom his favor rests" for en anthropois eudokias:** Sinaiticus reads eudokias (genitive), meaning "among people of [God's] good pleasure/favor," not simply "goodwill toward people." This is a significant textual point — the peace is directed toward those whom God has chosen to favor.

5. **Verse 19 — "turning them over carefully in her heart" for symballousa en te kardia autes:** The verb symballein means to bring together, compare, ponder — it is an active, reflective process, not passive storage. "Turning them over carefully" conveys this thoughtful meditation.

6. **Verse 29 — "releasing your servant" for apolyeis ton doulon sou:** The verb apolyein (release, let go) is a gentle euphemism for death. "Releasing" preserves the metaphor of a servant dismissed from duty, conveying both peace and finality.

7. **Verse 34 — "fiercely opposed" for antilegomenon:** More than simply "spoken against," this word carries the force of active, hostile contradiction. "Fiercely opposed" communicates the intensity of the resistance Jesus would face.

8. **Verse 35 — "a sword will pierce your own soul" for rhomphaia:** The rhomphaia is not a small blade but a large, broad sword — a weapon of devastating force. The image is of Mary's soul being run through by grief, not merely pricked.

9. **Verse 48 — "worried sick" for odynomenoi:** The Greek odynaomai means to be in anguish or torment. "Worried sick" is the natural English idiom for parental distress and captures the emotional force without exaggeration.

10. **Verse 49 — "my Father's house" for en tois tou patros mou:** This phrase is ambiguous in Greek — it could mean "in my Father's house" or "about my Father's business/affairs." I follow "house" because the temple setting makes it the more natural reading, though the ambiguity is worth noting.

— *PNEUMA*
